title: The Older-Adult Wellness Visit: Immunizations, Screening, and Healthy Aging
subtitle: A healthy, independent 72-year-old woman comes in for an annual wellness visit. The nurse updates immunizations and screenings, assesses function and medications, and counsels on healthy aging — individualizing care to her health, not just her age.

patient.name: Gloria Reyes
patient.admitDate: Primary-care clinic for an annual wellness visit. Independent and active, hypertension controlled on one agent, no acute complaints. Here to review immunizations, screenings, function, and medications.

vitals.temp: Missed screening; a fall; med side effects

nursesNote: Gloria Reyes, 72, here for an annual wellness visit: independent, active, hypertension controlled. The visit reviews due immunizations (influenza, pneumococcal, recombinant zoster, Td/Tdap), recommended screenings (bone density for women ≥65, colorectal through 75, depression, fall risk), functional status (ADLs/IADLs, gait, vision, hearing), and a full medication reconciliation for polypharmacy. Counseling targets exercise (strength + balance), nutrition, home safety, and advance care planning. Care is individualized to her health and life expectancy — not her age alone.
providerOrders: Update due immunizations (influenza, pneumococcal, recombinant zoster, Td/Tdap)
Arrange recommended screenings: bone density, colorectal, depression, fall risk
Assess function (ADLs/IADLs), gait, vision, and hearing
Reconcile all medications and OTCs; review for polypharmacy/deprescribing
Counsel on exercise, nutrition, home safety, and advance care planning

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does an older-adult wellness visit include?

opts.text: It reviews immunizations, screenings, and functional status
Screening decisions consider overall health and life expectancy, not age alone
Older adults benefit from regular exercise, including strength and balance
Reviewing all medications and OTCs catches polypharmacy and risky drugs
Being over 65 means preventive care and screening should simply stop
Functional assessment, vision, hearing, and gait are part of the visit
Adults ≥65 should receive an annual influenza vaccine
The recombinant zoster (shingles) vaccine is recommended for older adults
Women ≥65 should be screened for osteoporosis with bone-density testing
Colorectal cancer screening is generally recommended through age 75
Live attenuated vaccines are always preferred in immunocompromised elders
Depression and fall-risk screening are important in older adults

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the components of the visit

opts.text: Review history, current medications, and functional status
Update due immunizations and recommended screenings
Screen for falls, depression, cognition, vision, and hearing
Counsel on exercise/nutrition/safety; reconcile and deprescribe meds
Discuss advance care planning and schedule follow-up

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Recommended at this visit, or not?

opts.text: Give the annual influenza vaccine
Offer the recombinant zoster (Shingrix) series
Provide pneumococcal vaccination per the current schedule
Arrange a DEXA bone-density screen for this 72-year-old woman
Continue colorectal cancer screening at age 72
Perform depression and fall-risk screening
Skip all screening simply because she is "old"
Continue every medication indefinitely without review
Assume she needs no exercise because of her age

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Complete a thorough assessment including a medication review
Administer or arrange due vaccines and screenings
Provide individualized counseling on exercise, nutrition, and home safety
Address any positive screen (falls, mood, cognition) with referrals
Document, reinforce teaching, and plan follow-up
She receives her flu and shingles vaccines and an osteoporosis screen
She starts a strength and balance exercise program
Her medications are reconciled and a risky drug is deprescribed
She completes advance care planning with her family
Screening is skipped because the nurse assumes she is "too old"
A positive fall-risk and depression screen are ignored
She is told exercise is pointless at her age
All medications are continued without review, raising side effects

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
